gran_impulso_ambiental_concreto.evidencia: Test 1 (paquete masivo y coordinado): ausente. El brief propone medidas sectoriales de resiliencia y transición eléctrica (IRRP, renovables, estándares, atracción de inversión privada), no un paquete simultáneo de inversiones/políticas a escala de modelo de desarrollo. Test 2 (sectores estratégicos): presente de forma sectorial — transición hacia renovables, almacenamiento, mini/microgrids y smart grids, con instrumentos nombrados (IRRP avalado por CARICOM; actualización de renewable energy targets; estándares/códigos). Test 3 (tres eficiencias): solo la ambiental es clara (resiliencia climática y renovables); no desarrolla eficiencia keynesiana (empleo/desigualdad) ni schumpeteriana como eje de transformación productiva. Con instrumentos concretos pero sectoriales y sin lógica de paquete coordinado → Parcial.

citas.cita: Scale up and accelerate the incorporation of renewable energy sources into the electricity generation mix as a key climate adaptation measure, updating expired renewable energy targets when required.

Review and update the power sector’s policies, institutional arrangements, legislation, and regulations to attract private investments and competition to finance the additional costs required by building climate resilience in the power sector.

CARICOM member States endorsed the IRRP as a preferred mechanism to guide planning in the electricity sector. [...] Belize, Guyana, Jamaica, Saint Kitts and Nevis, and Trinidad and Tobago have already engaged with the Caribbean Centre for Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency (CREEE) Climate Resilience Programme to develop IRRPs.

articulacion_actores.evidencia: El documento nombra el Integrated Resource and Resilience Plan (IRRP) como mecanismo de planificación con nombre propio, avalado por los Estados miembros de CARICOM e implementándose en cinco países del ámbito (Belize, Guyana, Jamaica, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Trinidad and Tobago) con apoyo del CCREEE. Su proceso integra formalmente a actores más allá del ente rector energético (autoridades ambientales, oficinas meteorológicas, agencias de emergencia, prestadores de servicios públicos, planificadores urbanos). Cumple la regla de mecanismo de planificación multiactor adoptado institucionalmente dentro del ámbito de aplicación.

citas.cita: Building on the traditional integrated resource plan, the Integrated Resource and Resilience Plan (IRRP) is one method that brings the resilience element into the sector's planning process. CARICOM member States endorsed the IRRP as a preferred mechanism to guide planning in the electricity sector. [...] Belize, Guyana, Jamaica, Saint Kitts and Nevis, and Trinidad and Tobago have already engaged with the Caribbean Centre for Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency (CREEE) Climate Resilience Programme to develop IRRPs.

This critical step calls for close integration with stakeholders, such as environmental authorities, meteorological offices, national emergency agencies, public service providers, urban and physical planning practitioners, and others that can provide additional data.

Methodologies that mainstream climate resilience into traditional power sector planning — such as the Integrated Resource and Resilience Plans (IRRP) — are the ideal platform to promote a data-driven and multi-stakeholder collaborative dialogue.

oportunidades_productivas_sostenibles.evidencia: Chequeo negativo explícito: (a) el documento describe tecnologías y soluciones (renovables, almacenamiento, microgrids, smart grids, hardening) como medidas de resiliencia/adaptación del sector eléctrico, no como enumeración de oportunidades productivas sostenibles (sectores, industrias o mercados nuevos). (b) Se buscó vínculo desarrollado con empleo, productividad o reducción de desigualdad: no aparece. Hay menciones de impactos socioeconómicos de apagones, tarifas altas (US$ 0.33/kWh) y atracción de inversión privada para financiar costos de resiliencia, pero sin conectar la transición a empleo, productividad o desigualdad como oportunidad. Tampoco hay cifras propias ni instrumentos de política laboral/productiva asociados a esas tecnologías.

citas.cita: This section explores technologies and technical solutions that can be considered by governments, utilities, communities, individuals, and businesses when assessing cost-effective and least-regret measures to improve Caribbean SIDS' power sector climate resilience.
That dependency on imported fossil fuels leads to extremely high tariffs in the subregion, averaging US$ 0.33/kWh — three times higher than the average in the USA (Rozenberg and others, 2021).

como_hacerlo_concreto.evidencia: Desglose sobre el texto completo de la sección Policy recommendations (pp. 9–10), no sobre el recuadro Key recommendations de portada. De 8 ítems, 6 pasan el test de concreción (objeto identificable: mantenimiento en estrategias de DRR; mapa de infraestructura crítica; estadísticas/datos energéticos y de desastres; renovables + targets vencidos; estándares/códigos/regulaciones; políticas/legislación para inversión privada). Fallan 2 cuyo objeto es un principio/proceso abstracto (mainstream resiliencia en la planificación; enfoque multiactor/diálogo sin instrumento nombrado en el ítem).

citas.cita: Map and prioritize the power needs of critical infrastructure for disaster risk response and recovery strategies, such as telecommunications, hospitals and health facilities, water supply and sanitation, refrigeration for the food sector, shelters, and transportation.
Scale up and accelerate the incorporation of renewable energy sources into the electricity generation mix as a key climate adaptation measure, updating expired renewable energy targets when required.

Incorporate climate resilience into the energy industry’s quality standards, building codes, and other sectoral regulations, employing national research and development institutions and capabilities to explore and test the suitability and cost-effectiveness of technologies and technical solutions.

desglose_items.item: Include improved and regular maintenance of power infrastructure as a priority measure in disaster risk reduction and preparedness strategies.
Mainstream climate resilience into traditional power sector planning, considering the multi-hazardous nature of vulnerabilities along generation, transmission and distribution, and demand.

Map and prioritize the power needs of critical infrastructure for disaster risk response and recovery strategies (telecommunications, hospitals, water/sanitation, food refrigeration, shelters, transportation).

Strengthen human and institutional capacities to collect, process, analyze and disseminate energy statistics and climate disaster-related data, including spatialized risks and platforms to fill data gaps.

Promote a collaborative multiple-stakeholder approach / dialogue between traditional energy players and other stakeholders (environmental authorities, meteorological offices, emergency agencies, etc.).
Scale up and accelerate the incorporation of renewable energy sources into the electricity generation mix, updating expired renewable energy targets when required.

Incorporate climate resilience into the energy industry’s quality standards, building codes, and other sectoral regulations, employing national R&D institutions to test suitability and cost-effectiveness.
Review and update the power sector’s policies, institutional arrangements, legislation, and regulations to attract private investments and competition to finance climate resilience costs.

como_hacerlo_concreto.tally: 6 de 8 ítems pasan el test de concreción

tipologia.justificacion_breve: El brief trata la vulnerabilidad del power sector de los Caribbean SIDS frente a hurricanes y multi-hazard risks, y propone mainstream climate resilience en la planificación eléctrica junto con renovables como adaptation measure. La tensión que procesa es cómo fortalecer energy security y resiliencia climática durante la energy transition. Las recomendaciones concretas —IRRP, energy statistics y climate disaster-related data, standards/codes/regulations, institutional arrangements— apuntan a capacidades institucionales de planificación y regulación más que a un rediseño productivo amplio.
